content.problemStatement:
Chef has many accounts, each with a name and a list of email addresses. However, some of these accounts actually belong to the same person if they share at least one common email address. 

Chef wants to merge such accounts so that each person’s information is consolidated into a single account entry. Even if two accounts have the same name, they might represent different people unless they share an email. 

After merging, Chef wants each merged account to start with the person’s name followed by all unique email addresses sorted lexicographically. Help Chef perform this merging efficiently.

Function DeclarationFunction NamemergeAccountsmergeAccountsmergeAccounts - This function merges multiple account entries that share common email addresses into consolidated accounts.

Parameters
accountListsaccountListsaccountLists : A list of accounts, where each account is represented as array of strings.

The first element of each account is the account name.
The subsequent elements are one or more email addresses associated with that account.


Return Value
Returns a array of strings of merged accounts.
Each merged account contains the account name followed by a sorted list of unique email addresses.
All accounts sharing at least one common email are consolidated into a single account entry.
Constraints
1≤T≤1031 \leq T \leq 10^31≤T≤103
1≤N≤10001 \leq N \leq 10001≤N≤1000
2≤2 \leq2≤ length of each account (including name and emails) ≤10\leq 10≤10
1≤1 \leq1≤ length of each name and email ≤30\leq 30≤30
Names and emails consist only of English letters, digits, '@', and '.' characters.
Emails are guaranteed to be valid and unique within an account but may appear in multiple accounts.

Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.
For each test case:

The first line contains an integer NNN — the number of accounts Chef has.
The following NNN lines each describe an account:

The first string is the account name.
The next strings on the same line are the email addresses associated with that account.






Output Format

For each test case, print the merged accounts.
Each merged account should be printed on a new line starting with the account name followed by the sorted unique emails separated by spaces.
The order of merged accounts in the output does not matter.
constraints: 2 ≤ length of each account (including name and emails) ≤ 10
1 ≤ length of each name and email ≤ 30
Names and emails consist only of English letters, digits, '@', and '.' characters.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains a single integer N, the number of accounts. The following N lines each describe an account: The first string is the account name. The next strings on the same line are the email addresses associated with that account.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the merged accounts. Each merged account should be printed on a new line starting with the account name followed by the sorted unique emails separated by spaces.

sampleTestCases.input:
2
3
John john.doe@mail.com john.doe123@mail.com
John john.doe123@mail.com johnny@mail.com
Jane jane@mail.com
4
Alex alex@mail.com
Alex alex123@mail.com
Alex alex@mail.com alex123@mail.com
Bob bob@mail.com
sampleTestCases.output:
John john.doe123@mail.com john.doe@mail.com johnny@mail.com
Jane jane@mail.com
Bob bob@mail.com
Alex alex123@mail.com alex@mail.com
sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1: The first two John accounts share "john.doe123@mail.com", so they merge into one with all three emails combined. Jane's account is separate with no overlapping emails.
Test case 2: The first two Alex accounts have distinct emails, but the third includes both emails, merging all Alex accounts into one. Bob's account remains separate.

sampleTestCases.input:
1
5
Sam sam1@mail.com sam2@mail.com
Sam sam3@mail.com
Sam sam2@mail.com sam4@mail.com
Chris chris@mail.com
Sam sam5@mail.com
sampleTestCases.output:
Chris chris@mail.com
Sam sam1@mail.com sam2@mail.com sam4@mail.com
Sam sam3@mail.com
Sam sam5@mail.com
sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
The first, third accounts share sam2@mail.com, so they merge into one with emails sam1@mail.com, sam2@mail.com, sam4@mail.com.
The second and fifth accounts have unique emails, so they remain separate.
The fourth account belongs to Chris and has a unique email, so it remains unchanged.

Explanation:
The two John accounts have distinct emails, but since they have the same name, they merge into one account with both emails.

solution.approach: We use a Disjoint Set Union (DSU) data structure to group accounts that share at least one common email address. We iterate through each account and its associated emails. If an email is found to be associated with another account, we union the two accounts in the DSU. Finally, we group the emails by their root parent in the DSU and print the merged accounts.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N * M * alpha(N)) per test case, where N is the number of accounts, M is the maximum number of emails per account, and alpha(N) is the inverse Ackermann function. The total time complexity is O(T * N * M * alpha(N)).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N + M) — we use a DSU of size N and store M emails.
